retrieved_docs.title: Quality Improvement -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: QI initiatives utilize several methods to plan and frame systemic change in healthcare. These include Lean, Six Sigma, and the Model for Improvement theories. Essential tools are also utilized in QI initiatives, including Pareto charts, Ishikawa diagrams, Shewhart charts, run charts, and scatter plots.

retrieved_docs.title: Change Management In Health Care -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Lippitt, expanding on Lewin’s original theory, developed the Phases of Change Theory, which includes the following change phases: Increasing awareness of the need for change Developing a relationship between the system and the change agent Defining the change problem Setting goals and action plans for achieving change Implementing the change Gaining staff acceptance and stabilizing the change Redefining the relationship of the change agent and the system [3] Kotter’s 8-Step Change Model, developed in 1995, includes the following steps for effective change management: Create a sense of urgency for change Form a guiding change team Create a vision and plan for change Communicate the changed vision and plan with stakeholders Enable action by removing barriers to change Generate short-term wins Build on the change Anchor the change in the organizational culture [3] Finally, Rogers’ Diffusion of Innovation Theory outlines the following 5 phases of change: [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Change Management In Health Care -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The answer may lie within the work of several change leaders and theorists. Although theories may seem abstract and impractical for direct healthcare practice, they are valuable for addressing common healthcare challenges. Lewin, an early change scholar, proposed a 3-step process to facilitate successful change. [2] Other theorists, such as Lippitt, Kotter, and Rogers, have built on Lewin’s original Planned Change Theory, contributing to a broader understanding of change management. Each theory has its unique strengths and weaknesses, but their commonalities can offer best practices for sustaining positive change (see Image. Sample Force Field Analysis in Change Management). Lewin’s Theory of Planned Change includes the following change stages: Unfreezing: Understanding the need for change Moving: Initiating the process of change Refreezing: Establishing a new status quo [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Nursing Professional Development Standards -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The revised standards include an updated systems practice model comprised of inputs, throughputs, and outputs. Rainford et al described the practice model and changes in the latest standards edition. Inputs include environmental scanning, the learner, the 7 NPD roles, and the NPD standards. Throughputs include the organizational mission and vision and NPD practitioners' 6 key areas of responsibility, which include Orientation and onboarding Competency management Education Role development Collaborative partnerships Inquiry [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Nursing Professional Development Standards -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Three years after the 2016 standards were published, Harper and Maloney identified that although the standards have promoted uniformity of titles and alignment with the scope of practice, the specialty needs more advocacy, research, and engagement work. [3] In an article about the development of the updated edition, they outlined some of the work done to inform the latest standards edition. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Accountable Care Organization -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term ACO was originally coined by researchers and policy experts to describe entities that consist of responsibly integrated healthcare providers who all are working toward achieving a common clinical goal and outcome: efficient, high-quality patient care while utilizing a common clinical pathway that incorporates principles of treatment and therapeutic modalities in a multifaceted provider setting. [3] There are three core Accountable Care Organizations principles: [4] Provider-led organizations with a strong base of primary care that is accountable for the quality and per capita costs Payments linked to improvement in quality and reduced costs Reliable and increasingly sophisticated measurement of performance to support improvement and provide confidence care is improved, and cost savings occur.

retrieved_docs.title: National Quality Forum -- Definition/Introduction -- Role of The NQF on Quality Improvement and Cost-effectiveness
retrieved_docs.content: Endorsing standardized performance measures: This approach involves pooling and prioritizing recommendations from various stakeholders such as the American Medical Association, the National Committee for Quality Assurance, Center for Medicare & Medicaid Services, and Joint Commission. The NQF uses distinct criteria to evaluate submitted metrics. Each proposed metric must meet the first and second criteria in the pyramid to be endorsed (Fig.1). In other words, an endorsed measure must demonstrate a high-impact health target or priority and must meet scientific standards. Also, measures should be relatively easy to use and tailor to specific healthcare settings with little burden. Hence, NQF endorsed measures are considered as a gold standard and granted privileged status in federal regulations. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Determinants of Health -- Summary / Explanation
retrieved_docs.content: In a seminal paper, Kindig and Stoddart defined population health as "the health outcomes of a group of individuals, including the distribution of such outcomes within the group." [1] This definition embodies both outcomes or results and determinants or contributing factors. Compared to a more traditional approach of focusing on individual factors such as genetics and lifestyle, population health embraces additional determinants that may include healthcare policies, public health, and social and physical milieu. [1] Population health determinants can serve as a preventative approach and address root causes rather than symptoms like a biomedical approach. [2] Results from a recent scoping review found 57 frameworks, or organizational structures, to define the components of population health. [2] In addition to individual health status and health behavior, other crucial factors were identified, including social determinants of health and healthcare system performance. [2] The following sections will discuss several determinants of health. These factors do not make up a complete list of population health determinants, and their categorization varies in the literature, they also do not exist in isolation but rather in a complex, interrelated matrix (ODPHP. Healthy People 2030 ). [1]

retrieved_docs.content: Governments effectively reduce these dangerous health factors by taxing tobacco products or preventing the advertisement of these harmful substances. For example, Hong Kong’s reduction in tobacco advertising was associated with a subsequent decline in tobacco consumption. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Quality Tools and Techniques (Fishbone Diagram, Pareto Chart, Process Map) -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: QI in healthcare involves a wide range of tools and strategies. Common QI methodologies include the Model for Improvement, Plan-Do-Study-Act, Lean, and Six Sigma. [6] These methodologies employ diverse QI tools to identify root causes, address process issues, prioritize change opportunities, and anticipate potential barriers ( AHRQ. QI Toolkit ).

retrieved_docs.title: Stark Law -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term "referral" means a request for a specific or service by a physician for Medicare Part B services and/or a care plan that includes designated health services. Also, the term "financial relationship" includes investment interest, ownership, and compensation arrangements.

retrieved_docs.title: Quality Improvement -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Quality initiatives and performance improvement efforts must clearly define the problem on which the quality initiative focuses. A single project goal should be defined, and it must be specific, measurable, achievable, realistic, and timely. [5] Defining the locations, processes, and disciplines involved in the QI initiative is imperative. Once a clear, concise problem statement and a hypothesis regarding the outcome of the proposed intervention are formed, it is often helpful to deliver a powerful emotional message among the stakeholders to motivate them and emphasize the importance of the quality initiative. A patient-centered approach that considers the patient's experience is central to all approaches to QI.

retrieved_docs.content: Pareto Chart: Stemming from the 80/20 Pareto principle that 20% of factors cause 80% of the problems, is a bar chart showing the frequencies of factors in descending order from left to right to easily identify the most important factors. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 977
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology is a categorical framework that incorporates several obstetric and perinatal concepts. Within its subset, it includes the Arabic terms مقدمات تسمم الحمل (preliminary signs of preeclampsia), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), and قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum), reflecting maternal conditions that can affect newborn health and screening outcomes. Additionally, the subset of Newborn Screening Terminology encompasses the concept الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين (delivery by fetal suction or vacuum‑assisted extraction), highlighting a specific mode of birth that may be relevant to newborn assessment and follow‑up. Together, these concepts illustrate how Newborn Screening Terminology integrates maternal disease states and delivery techniques to inform comprehensive newborn evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology is a defined set of terms that encompasses the concept plod and also includes the concept الولادة بالملقط, indicating that both of these items are part of its subset of screening-related terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is a form of elevated arterial pressure that is identified and interpreted through measurement of ضغط الدم (blood pressure). The concept of ضغط الدم includes the entire spectrum of arterial pressures and is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), illustrating the continuum from hypotension to hypertension. Recognizing how essential hypertension is defined by ضغط الدم and how ضغط الدم connects with ضغط الدم المنخفض helps clinicians assess, diagnose, and manage blood pressure disorders more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2321
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology comprises a range of specific concepts used in the evaluation of newborn health, and it includes both the Arabic term “تسمم الحمل” and the term “plod” as distinct elements within this specialized vocabulary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both listed as members of the Arabic‑named SMQ “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”, which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This classification indicates that, within this standardized medical terminology, these two distinct clinical categories are considered part of a broader collection of disorders that share underlying developmental, hereditary, or genetic characteristics. Recognizing their inclusion in the same SMQ can aid clinicians and researchers in organizing diagnostic codes, facilitating epidemiological studies, and ensuring comprehensive patient care that addresses both congenital urogenital conditions and infections transmitted through sexual contact under a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2450
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is a clinical condition that includes the standardized MedDRA query “ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ)”. The Arabic term “ضغط الدم” is used to interpret essential hypertension, indicating that blood pressure measurement is central to its definition. Moreover, within the Newborn Screening Terminology, the concept “ضغط الدم” is listed as a member of a specific subset, emphasizing the relevance of blood‑pressure assessment in newborn screening protocols. This linkage of essential hypertension, its Arabic descriptors, and the inclusion of blood‑pressure concepts in newborn screening illustrates the importance of consistent terminology across disease classification systems and pediatric screening practices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1575
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology encompasses a specific subset of concepts that includes تسمم الحمل, قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة, and plod. These entities represent distinct conditions or terms that are incorporated within the standardized language used for newborn screening, facilitating precise identification, classification, and communication of relevant neonatal health issues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2401
retrieved_docs.content: Previous c‑section and maternal care for other types of scar from a prior cesarean delivery are both classified under the term “عملية قيصرية سابقة,” which denotes a previous cesarean operation. This classification groups the English concepts of a prior c‑section and related maternal scar‑care together with the Arabic label, highlighting their shared relevance in obstetric history and maternal care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2407
retrieved_docs.content: In obstetric care, the broad category “other procedures to assist delivery” encompasses a more specific subgroup called “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery.” Within this subgroup, the Arabic‑named concept “فشل التوليد بالملقط” is identified as a child entity, indicating that it represents a particular type of procedure or complication related to assisted delivery. This hierarchical organization shows that “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery” is a child of “other procedures to assist delivery,” and “فشل التوليد بالملقط” is a child of “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery,” reflecting their interrelated roles in the classification of delivery‑assisting interventions.
